sentences.text: I remained in the ghetto and with my mother until the ghetto was liquidated.
And that was in 1943.
By that time, my father was already in camp.
Along with the-- with the ghetto, the Germans begin to build a camp just outside of Krakow.
Plaszow And some people were slowly being moved into Plaszow.
Those people who were building the camp, for example, already lived there.
Well, eventually, those people who remained in the ghetto, who survived the ghetto, were going to be transferred into this labor camp, and the ghetto was going to be liquidated.
Closed.
And so you know, a ghetto-- the ghetto was-- the purpose of the ghetto was not simply to isolate the Jews.
It was simply a place where Jews could be held together and be dealt with by the-- in the Nazi fashion more easily.
Well, the-- the final plan was to murder everyone.
That's simply-- that's just that.
It sounds--
Not-- not at the time.
The human mind can't quite absorb something like this.
I know it sounds-- it sounds very strange and-- and unbelievable when I say they were-- they were planning to murder everyone.
Well, the uncertainty was the-- was the worst part.
And the hope was that the war was going to be over before this could happen.
So-- so basically, as I said, the ghetto was simply a place-- a holding pen.

And the evidence, of course we know, is that immediately after the ghetto was created, where everyone was herded into it in-- in mass, they begin to take people out in transports and sending them somewhere else.
A German couple.
Their name was Lustig.
I thought they were two old people at the time.
But after thinking about it as an adult, they were in their fifties.
They must have-- Yes.
Not with them.
They had-- Lustig was a fine, refined gentleman who originally-- whose-- whose family originally came from Poland.
And he was-- oh, he was thrown out of Germany along with the other Jews and ended up in Poland.
And then he ended up in the Krakow ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He and his wife were by themselves.
I don't know the-- their Lustig's first name.
I never called--
Herr Lustig and Frau Lustig.
Very, very kind people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They didn't have any place to go.
And my mother was sort of like that.
You know, people there without a place to live, she just said, well, you come and live with us.
No.
Not really.
We just-- we only had one bedroom.
We separated the bedroom with some furniture and a-- a blanket across and let them take the one side-- one half of the bedroom.
Yes.
He was taken-- he and his wife were taken on one of those transports and were killed.
Probably Belzec.
We don't know whether there was Belzec or whether there was Majdanek, or one of those camps where they executed people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
It's very ironic that Mr.
Lustig, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when he was talking with me, he-- he told me that he had a son in-- in the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that after the war, he would take me to the United States.
No.
He didn't-- Yes.
But it was impossible.
I-- we don't know if even Lustig was the right name.
Whether his son kept Lustig as a last name.
No, they weren't.
They were reform.
German reform Jews.
Very kind, and as I said, refined.
I remember I liked to watch him because he smoked pipes, and he had a whole array of different kinds of pipes.
It was interesting to see.
But it's ironic that he promised to take me to-- to America.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he did, but he didn't make it.
He was taken on one of the transports.
There were several transports.
And as they began, they were always done with a lot of deception.
Germans were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] playing games.
The first transport ever taken out of the ghetto actually was one where they took people out to the countryside, and they had them do labor, and they weren't watched very carefully, and some people--
That was soon after the ghetto was created, in probably '41, or early  '41.
I-- I knew people who actually were taken on the transport and returned.
And they actually said, well, we just returned.
And they came back to their families.
But the subsequent transports never returned.
They were all taken to the extermination camps.
My father, during that period of time, was working in Schindler's company.
And Schindler had actually increased his Jewish workforce, so those people who lived in-- in the ghetto and marched out every day to go to work to Schindler's company and come back.
So he-- my father asked Schindler to-- if he could take my older brother.
My-- the one next to-- just next to me.
The one that's between--
David.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so Schindler did just that.
He actually accepted him.
And so at this point, my-- my father and my brother used to march out to go to Schindler's company and come back.
Oh, about 25 or 30.
And a group of them would all go out together and come back together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was-- I-- I got this job working in a brush factory.
The Germans had these factories that were inside the ghetto as well.
And so they-- we made brushes for the German army.
We made things like uniforms for the German army, boots and things like that.
Just brush factory.
[POLISH].
[POLISH]
Polish.
Jews did.
We all did.
Well, I was a-- I was a worker.
I was an employee.
I made brushes.
Oh, I was 12.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother was doing this cleaning stuff and working as a-- as a cleaning lady.
My sister was already in a-- in a camp, working on this [INAUDIBLE] And--
In the brush factory?
Until the end-- until the ghetto was liquidated.
That was '43, I believe. 1943.
Well, you know, I actually earned something from that.
You know, I-- I was paid.
I was-- I was given money, like a ghetto money, to buy things with.
Not really.
It was-- it looked more like [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] tickets of a kind.
Tickets for-- the kind you use for going to the theater.
Yes.
Uh-huh.
It did.
I don't recall exactly what it said, but it did have that it's a Jewish ghetto and so on.
Just exchange it among ourselves.
Yes, there were some, but that-- that was not-- was not worth very much for-- it was just exchange among us.
No.
But you know, all those things were prohibited.
But we had a cheder we went to.
Right behind their backs.
And we had schools, and--
It was in a building.
In a-- in a back of a building.
I don't recall the-- that teacher.
I recall my first one, but not this one.
But I--
I was studying.
I was really advancing.
I was learning Gemara and--
We used to go in the-- in the afternoon.
And of course there weren't any public schools or anything, but you know, we talk about this business about people not resisting and not running away and all this, but there was a resistance.
We did resist.
We-- we-- we-- taught ourselves.
People with talent performed.
We sang.
We created poems.
And there was entertainment.
People went to the synagogue, which was all prohibited, of course, under-- under penalty of death.
But they couldn't-- they couldn't suppress that part of the-- of the spirit.
Well, you know, what-- what-- what they-- what they did was they would-- we would take songs of-- popular songs of the time and add different lyrics to it.
Well, many of them were quite sad.
They were-- some of them were humorous, but the humor was gallows humor.
You know, that--
Yeah, I do.
But I can't sing it for you.
Maybe in a-- maybe after a little while I can, but not right now.
It was a-- I mean, there was a song about Treblinka.
Treblinka was a death camp.
Well, eventually, you know, when people found out the fact that transports were going to death camps.
Well, it was Treblinka, there, there is a place for every Jew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You can go in, but you cannot come out.
And--
In Yiddish.
Maybe.
Treblinka [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And it was set to a popular song at the time, [INAUDIBLE].
[SINGING IN YIDDISH]
No.
No.
Not really.
Not at the time.
However, you know I've already told you that we had a brother who was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] younger, next to the oldest one, who was also with us at the time.
And he was taken on one of those transports.
Bezalel.
Bezalel.
Probably Belzec.
But I was at home.
I was in the house, and my parents were in the house when he was dragged out.
The-- the Germans played these games where one had to have a special stamp.
And when one didn't have the stamp, then they would be taken on the transport.
And he didn't get the stamp, and so he was taken away.
The guards, the Germans, just came in and dragged him out.
We were petrified.
We said nothing.
No one said a word.
Absolutely.
We were all home and no one said-- we were just frozen.
It was a-- a very unreal experience.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And somehow from-- at the time, you know, it was so unbelievable that for years and years after that I thought that he-- he might be still alive.
That I would meet him some place.
Mm-hm.
We never did.
We never did find out for absolutely certain.
Just that he never reappeared.
And we know that those transports were going directly to the death camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there's an incident in a movie called where Schindler goes to retrieve his accountant, because he was taken on a transport by mistake.
He didn't have his papers.
And so Schindler goes to-- to the railroad station where the transport was being formed and finds his accountant and takes him off the train.
Well, that was the same transport that my brother was on.
And Schindler saw him on the transport and offered to take him off, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but he refused to take him.
My brother refused to get off the train, because he was there with his girlfriend, And Schindler couldn't get his girlfriend off.
He wouldn't leave without his girlfriend.
Yes he did.
Mm-hm.

And he-- and we know that's-- that's a fact, because Schindler had no way of knowing that my brother was there with his girlfriend, but we knew he was, because his girlfriend was the daughter of a brush maker who lived in our building.
Do you know, I can't remember her name, but I probably will after the interview.
But this is how we got connected with the brush factory where we-- I got a job working for brushes.
I had connections.
That's right.
My brother was taken away, and the ghetto was being shrunk.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people were being sent-- those people who had jobs outside-- were being sent to-- to Plaszow to live.
My brother and my father, who worked for Schindler, were then sent to Plaszow.
The camp was still in-- under construction, but the people were being sent there.
They were-- they were living there and leaving the camp and going to work with Schindler's company and going back.
At that point, my mother and I were the only ones left in the ghetto.
I was approaching 13-- I was 12.
I was 12, approaching 13.
Yes, uh-huh/
Well, sort of-- in the-- probably in the-- in the middle of the year, maybe towards-- towards, fall, the end of summer.
Well, the-- the ghetto was cut in half.
Actually, a part of the ghetto was separated, and everyone who remained was squeezed into a smaller area.
No.
We were moved.
We moved into a smaller area and then finally ended up living in an attic.
My mother and I ended up living in an attic.
You know, it's very difficult to say by this time, because it was so-- so unreal.
We were there for three or four months in the attic.
Yes, I remember exactly.
When the ghetto was split in half, and one of the-- one of the-- part of the ghetto was going to be eliminated, we lived on the-- on that side.
And so everyone who still lived on that side had to get out.
And I-- I remember the last thing my mother did was that she got a pot and-- and left, and got out.
But got out very late, the last minute, with this pot in her hand.
And that's-- that's the question everybody asks.
What are you doing with this?
But it came in handy.
She knew what she was doing.
Well, she would make-- boil potatoes and make soup and stuff like that.
I mean, she--
Yes.
She was the kind of person that was very good under pressure, in the immediate emergency.
She did all these things in the emergency.
And she was the last one to leave, and she would pick up something, and somebody would say, oh, leave that.
And then next-- next time, we needed it.
So finally-- but when the pressure was off, she'd just come apart.

Oh, She would be practically hysterical about what was going to happen and what happened to-- to my older brother, and what happened to-- where is Aviva, and all these things that just came pouring out.
Somewhat.
But you know, I only came to this understanding later, to this-- to this better understanding.
I-- I understood a little bit more about what my parents went through during that period of time when my children were the age that I was then.
And so when I think about that, I can't even imagine what I would do if my children and I were in that-- in that situation.
I don't think I could have-- I could have coped with that, not knowing where my son was at age 13.
He was an excellent soccer player, you know, and played Little League and basketball.
And you know, I-- it's just inconceivable.
Consciously not.
I try not to include that part of my life.
I really consider myself somebody with two lives, you know.
The one I left behind in 1949 when I came to this country, and the one I had before that.
No.
But I-- I think I was a kind of a father like my father was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted them to succeed-- my children to succeed, and I want them to do well.
But I would not reprimand them for not succeeding in something that came.
And as it turned out, both my children turned out very-- quite well, I must say.
My-- my daughter is-- went to UCSD, to--
To school.
And graduated and met a Navy pilot when she was in San Diego.
I understand that.
Yeah.
But anyway, that's the kind of a father I was.
But I couldn't-- I-- I must say that reaching the understanding-- the kind of understanding I have now about how my parents suffered only came later.
In my-- in my life--
Well, before-- when-- when the last transport was being made-- and we always knew when this was going to happen, because all these special troops came and surrounded the ghetto.
That was just before the ghetto was liquidated, in '43.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we knew the-- the modus operandi by that time, so we knew that something was up.
And my father was already off to the factory and-- with his working group.
And my-- Aviva was-- my sister was already at her working group.
And my mother and I were the only ones left.
And the plan was-- my mother was planning that I was going to hide.
I was going to hide someplace.
Because at this point, there was no chance of being exempted from the transport.
So in between our two buildings where we lived, there was a-- in the courtyard, there was a small-- small building.
As a matter of fact, boy, we-- the brush maker lived there.
But above that was a small attic, but it didn't look like it would have an attic, because it was sort of like a lean-to in between-- in the courtyard.
But when we were small kids, we used to play there, and crawl in.
There was a little opening on the side, on the very side of the building.
Not very visible.
So my mother decided that that's-- that's where I was going to hide with two of my friends who also lived in the building.
They were-- they belonged to the shoemaker who lived there.
Birch.
I remember him.
I used to hang around the shoemaker's house quite a bit.
That I can't remember.
Because I had some other friends also that age from-- from another building.
In any case, the two children of the Birch family and myself crawled up into this attic space, which was only high enough to lie down but not sit up.
But the building was quite long, so we crawled all the way back to the back of the building.
And we began to hear the noises of the Germans running around and hauling people out and everything.
The mother of the two friends was going to act as if she were in charge of the building and be sweeping the grounds or something, and she thought maybe that would help.
Oh yes.
I can't tell.
I mean, we knew that the surround-- the ghetto was surrounded by these special troops.
There was only one thing that was going to happen.
She was sweeping the grounds.
Well, and my mother wasn't going to hide.
You know, she was just going to take her chances and probably be shipped off.
But she remembered that we were up there, and we didn't have anything to eat or drink.
Well, we didn't take anything with us.
We ended up there three days.
It could have been three months, I don't know.
But it's really three days.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she filled up a-- a teapot, a regular kind of normal teapot, full of water.
And she was carrying it across the yard to hand it to us.
And we could see her, because there was-- the building was not very well made, and there was some cracks in the-- in the roof, so we could see.
And as she was coming up, the guards broke into the yard and started yelling for those people to get out.
And she was just about to hand us the teapot.
So she set the teapot down, and just by instinct, just crawled up into this crawl space and crawled in with us, left the teapot on the ground.
And the Germans were running around, and they're yelling.
They hauled the woman, the mother of those two children, out.
There was a lot of screaming and yelling.
And we lay there for some time while this was going on.
And then when nightfall came, things began to quiet down.
We could just hear sporadic shootings and yelling.
And then finally, it got quiet.
And we just laid there.
We didn't know what to do.
We were afraid to come out.
We didn't know what was happening.
And so eventually, the following day, somebody did come, came into the yard.
And-- and said, OK.
It's ready.
You know, you can come out now.
It's safe.
But by that time, we had too many tricks and too many deceptions, so we decided that maybe we shouldn't come out.
So we stayed there until somebody came the following day and called us by name.
My father had sent someone and tried-- he didn't know whether we survived or not.
But he thought if by some chance if we did, if he called us by name, maybe we would come out.
And we did.
